question: [File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Critical Multicall or sequencing bug that breaks invariants only when multiple protocol actions are composed in one transaction'] [Function: cancelRoot + take] Can an unprivileged maker under the precondition that they hold a valid signed Merkle-root offer tree trigger a multicall([cancelRoot(maker, root), take(offer, ratifierData, ...)]) sequence, violating the invariant that ratifier approval must bind the correct maker/root at the time of fill, causing scoped impact: the take succeeds despite the root being canceled in the same transaction because cancelRoot is ordered after take in the multicall, allowing the maker to atomically fill-then-cancel and prevent any competing taker from filling the same root in a later block? Proof idea: fuzz multicall call ordering (cancelRoot before vs after take) and assert that isRootCanceled[maker][root] == true after the multicall while consumed[maker][group] > 0.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Critical Multicall or sequencing bug that breaks invariants only when multiple protocol actions are composed in one transaction'] [Function: take + cancelRoot (same root, same multicall)] Can an unprivileged taker under the precondition that a valid EcrecoverRatifier-backed offer exists trigger a multicall([take(offer, ratifierData, units), cancelRoot(maker, root)]) sequence, violating the invariant that cancelled offers cannot be filled, causing scoped impact: the taker fills the offer and atomically cancels the root in the same transaction, permanently blocking any subsequent partial fill of the same root by other takers? Proof idea: unit test that after multicall([take, cancelRoot]), a second standalone take with the same ratifierData reverts with RootCanceled.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Critical Multicall or sequencing bug that breaks invariants only when multiple protocol actions are composed in one transaction'] [Function: setIsAuthorized + take (ratifier authorization)] Can an unprivileged maker under the precondition that offer.ratifier is not yet authorized trigger a multicall([setIsAuthorized(ratifier, true, onBehalf=maker), take(offer, ratifierData, ...)]) sequence, violating the invariant that isAuthorized[offer.maker][offer.ratifier] must be true before take is called, causing scoped impact: the maker atomically authorizes the EcrecoverRatifier and fills the offer in one transaction, bypassing any window where the ratifier was unauthorized and preventing front-running of the authorization? Proof idea: assert that a standalone take before the multicall reverts with RatifierUnauthorized, but the multicall succeeds.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Critical Multicall or sequencing bug that breaks invariants only when multiple protocol actions are composed in one transaction'] [Function: setIsAuthorized + take + setIsAuthorized (grant-use-revoke)] Can an unprivileged maker under the precondition that a signer address is not authorized trigger a multicall([setIsAuthorized(signer, true, onBehalf=maker), take(offer_signed_by_signer, ratifierData, ...), setIsAuthorized(signer, false, onBehalf=maker)]) sequence, violating the invariant that ratifier approval must bind the correct authorized signer at fill time, causing scoped impact: the offer is filled using a signer whose authorization is revoked by end of block, making post-hoc authorization audits misleading and allowing one-shot signer delegation without persistent state? Proof idea: assert isAuthorized[maker][signer] == false after multicall while consumed[maker][group] > 0.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Critical Multicall or sequencing bug that breaks invariants only when multiple protocol actions are composed in one transaction'] [Function: isRatified (view reads isAuthorized mid-multicall)] Can an unprivileged operator authorized by the maker under the precondition that the operator is authorized trigger a multicall([setIsAuthorized(newSigner, true, onBehalf=maker), take(offer_signed_by_newSigner, ratifierData, ...)]) sequence, violating the invariant that ratifier approval must bind the correct maker and signer at the time of the offer's creation, causing scoped impact: the operator grants a new signer authorization and immediately fills an offer signed by that signer in one atomic transaction, allowing the operator to expand the set of valid signers and fill offers without the maker's per-transaction consent? Proof idea: fuzz newSigner addresses and assert that isRatified returns CALLBACK_SUCCESS only when isAuthorized[maker][newSigner] is true at call time.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Critical Multicall or sequencing bug that breaks invariants only when multiple protocol actions are composed in one transaction'] [Function: take (maker callback) + cancelRoot] Can an unprivileged maker under the precondition that the offer has a maker callback set trigger a take where the onBuy/onSell callback calls cancelRoot(maker, root) mid-execution, violating the invariant that the ratifier check (isRootCanceled read) must remain consistent throughout the take execution, causing scoped impact: the take succeeds (ratifier was called before callbacks), but the root is canceled atomically within the same transaction, preventing any other taker from filling any other offer in the same Merkle tree in a subsequent call? Proof idea: implement a callback contract that calls EcrecoverRatifier.cancelRoot and assert isRootCanceled[maker][root] == true after take returns.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Critical Multicall or sequencing bug that breaks invariants only when multiple protocol actions are composed in one transaction'] [Function: take (maker callback) + setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged maker under the precondition that the offer has a maker callback set trigger a take where the onBuy callback calls setIsAuthorized(signer, false, onBehalf=maker) mid-execution, violating the invariant that authorization state must be consistent between the ratifier check and the end of the take, causing scoped impact: the take succeeds (ratifier already validated signer authorization), but the signer's authorization is revoked within the same transaction, leaving the protocol state inconsistent with the authorization that was used to validate the fill? Proof idea: assert isAuthorized[maker][signer] == false after take while consumed[maker][group] > 0.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Critical Multicall or sequencing bug that breaks invariants only when multiple protocol actions are composed in one transaction'] [Function: multicall + take (two fills of same offer)] Can an unprivileged taker under the precondition that an EcrecoverRatifier-backed offer has maxUnits > 0 and remaining capacity trigger a multicall([take(offer, ratifierData, units1), take(offer, ratifierData, units2)]) sequence, violating the invariant that offer fill amount cannot exceed remaining offer capacity, causing scoped impact: if consumed accounting is updated correctly between the two delegatecall frames, the second take correctly reverts with ConsumedUnits; but if any state isolation bug exists in delegatecall context, both fills succeed and the offer is overfilled? Proof idea: invariant fuzz test asserting consumed[maker][group] <= offer.maxUnits after any multicall sequence.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Critical Multicall or sequencing bug that breaks invariants only when multiple protocol actions are composed in one transaction'] [Function: multicall + take (two fills of same assets-based offer)] Can an unprivileged taker under the precondition that an EcrecoverRatifier-backed offer has maxAssets > 0 trigger a multicall([take(offer, ratifierData, units1), take(offer, ratifierData, units2)]) sequence, violating the invariant that consumed[maker][group] <= offer.maxAssets, causing scoped impact: if buyerAssets rounding in the two delegatecall frames accumulates differently than a single fill, the total consumed assets could exceed maxAssets by rounding dust, allowing the offer to be overfilled by a small amount? Proof idea: fuzz units1 and units2 near maxAssets boundary and assert consumed[maker][group] <= offer.maxAssets after multicall.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Critical Multicall or sequencing bug that breaks invariants only when multiple protocol actions are composed in one transaction'] [Function: multicall + take (cross-market shared group)] Can an unprivileged taker under the precondition that two EcrecoverRatifier-backed offers in different markets share the same offer.group trigger a multicall([take(offerMarketA, ratifierData, units1), take(offerMarketB, ratifierData, units2)]) sequence, violating the invariant that the consumption group budget cannot be exceeded across offers, causing scoped impact: if the ratifier validates each offer independently without checking cross-market consumed state, the taker fills both offers in one transaction and the total consumed exceeds the group budget? Proof idea: assert consumed[maker][group] <= min(offerA.maxUnits, offerB.maxUnits) after multicall with shared group.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Critical Multicall or sequencing bug that breaks invariants only when multiple protocol actions are composed in one transaction'] [Function: setConsumed + take (group reset attempt)] Can an unprivileged maker under the precondition that consumed[maker][group] > 0 trigger a multicall([setConsumed(group, type(uint256).max, onBehalf=maker), take(offer, ratifierData, ...)]) sequence, violating the invariant that cancelled offers cannot be filled, causing scoped impact: setting consumed to type(uint256).max cancels the group, and the subsequent take in the same multicall reverts with ConsumedUnits; but if the ordering is reversed (take then setConsumed), the take succeeds and the group is atomically canceled, preventing any future fills? Proof idea: test both orderings and assert that take-then-setConsumed(max) succeeds while setConsumed(max)-then-take reverts.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Critical Multicall or sequencing bug that breaks invariants only when multiple protocol actions are composed in one transaction'] [Function: take (sell) + withdrawCollateral (same multicall)] Can an unprivileged borrower under the precondition that they have collateral deposited and a valid EcrecoverRatifier-backed sell offer trigger a multicall([take(sellOffer, ratifierData, units), withdrawCollateral(market, collateralIndex, assets, onBehalf, receiver)]) sequence, violating the invariant that collateral cannot be withdrawn if it would make debt unhealthy, causing scoped impact: the borrower atomically borrows and withdraws collateral in one transaction; the health check in withdrawCollateral must catch the post-borrow unhealthy state, but if the debt increase from take is not reflected in the position storage before withdrawCollateral reads it, the withdrawal succeeds leaving an undercollateralized position? Proof idea: assert isHealthy(market, id, borrower) == true after multicall.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Critical Multicall or sequencing bug that breaks invariants only when multiple protocol actions are composed in one transaction'] [Function: take (sell) + liquidate (same multicall)] Can an unprivileged attacker under the precondition that a borrower has a near-threshold position and a valid EcrecoverRatifier-backed sell offer trigger a multicall([take(sellOffer_for_borrower, ratifierData, units), liquidate(market, collateralIndex, seizedAssets, 0, borrower, false, receiver, 0, '')]) sequence, violating the invariant that only unhealthy accounts should be liquidatable, causing scoped impact: the attacker forces the borrower into an unhealthy state via the take and immediately liquidates in the same transaction, seizing collateral before the borrower can react? Proof idea: assert that the borrower's position is unhealthy after the take and that the liquidation succeeds in the same multicall.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Critical Multicall or sequencing bug that breaks invariants only when multiple protocol actions are composed in one transaction'] [Function: take (buy) + withdraw (same multicall)] Can an unprivileged lender under the precondition that withdrawable < units and a valid EcrecoverRatifier-backed buy offer exists trigger a multicall([take(buyOffer, ratifierData, units), withdraw(market, units, onBehalf, receiver)]) sequence, violating the invariant that contract token balances must cover withdrawable credit redemptions, causing scoped impact: the lender atomically acquires credit and immediately withdraws it in one transaction; if the withdrawable accounting is not updated atomically between the take and withdraw delegatecall frames, the withdraw could succeed even when the market has insufficient withdrawable assets? Proof idea: assert withdrawable(id) >= 0 and token balance >= withdrawable after multicall.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Critical Multicall or sequencing bug that breaks invariants only when multiple protocol actions are composed in one transaction'] [Function: take + repay + withdraw (same multicall)] Can an unprivileged user under the precondition that they have both credit and debt in a market trigger a multicall([take(sellOffer, ratifierData, units), repay(market, units, onBehalf, 0, ''), withdraw(market, creditUnits, onBehalf, receiver)]) sequence, violating the invariant that total credit, debt, and token balances must stay solvent, causing scoped impact: the user borrows, repays, and withdraws credit in one transaction; if the repay increases withdrawable and the withdraw immediately drains it, the net effect could leave the market with insufficient token balance to cover remaining credit claims? Proof idea: invariant test asserting token.balanceOf(midnight) >= withdrawable(id) + claimableSettlementFee[loanToken] after any multicall sequence.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Critical Multicall or sequencing bug that breaks invariants only when multiple protocol actions are composed in one transaction'] [Function: flashLoan + take (same multicall)] Can an unprivileged taker under the precondition that they lack assets to fund a take trigger a multicall([flashLoan([loanToken], [assets], callback, data), take(offer, ratifierData, units, taker, receiver, takerCallback, takerCallbackData)]) sequence, violating the invariant that ERC20 transfer deltas must match accounting deltas, causing scoped impact: the flash loan provides assets to fund the take in the same transaction; if the flash loan repayment and take payment interact incorrectly with the token balance accounting, the protocol could end up with fewer tokens than expected? Proof idea: assert token.balanceOf(midnight) is unchanged after multicall([flashLoan, take]) net of the take's fee.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Critical Multicall or sequencing bug that breaks invariants only when multiple protocol actions are composed in one transaction'] [Function: take (callback reentrancy via multicall)] Can an unprivileged taker under the precondition that the takerCallback is a contract that calls midnight.multicall trigger a take where the onSell callback calls multicall([take(sameOffer, ratifierData, units2)]), violating the invariant that multicall/callback/reentrancy must not break invariants that hold for single calls, causing scoped impact: the reentrant take inside the callback executes before the outer take's health check and liquidation lock release, potentially allowing the seller to be liquidated while the liquidation lock is set or allowing double-fill of the offer? Proof idea: implement a reentrant callback and assert consumed[maker][group] <= offer.maxUnits and liquidationLocked(id, seller) == false after the outer take returns.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Critical Multicall or sequencing bug that breaks invariants only when multiple protocol actions are composed in one transaction'] [Function: take (callback calls cancelRoot mid-take)] Can an unprivileged taker under the precondition that the takerCallback is a contract that calls EcrecoverRatifier.cancelRoot trigger a take where the onBuy callback calls cancelRoot(maker, root), violating the invariant that ratifier approval must bind the correct maker/root at fill time, causing scoped impact: the ratifier check passes (root not yet canceled), the callback cancels the root, and the take completes successfully; a second take of a different offer in the same Merkle tree in the same multicall after the callback would fail, but the first take's fill is already committed? Proof idea: assert isRootCanceled[maker][root] == true and consumed[maker][group] > 0 after take with canceling callback.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Critical Multicall or sequencing bug that breaks invariants only when multiple protocol actions are composed in one transaction'] [Function: take (callback calls setIsAuthorized to grant new signer)] Can an unprivileged taker under the precondition that the takerCallback is a contract that calls setIsAuthorized trigger a take where the onBuy callback calls setIsAuthorized(newSigner, true, onBehalf=maker), violating the invariant that authorization must only allow intended account delegation, causing scoped impact: the callback expands the maker's authorized signer set mid-take, and a subsequent take in the same multicall using an offer signed by newSigner succeeds because isAuthorized[maker][newSigner] is now true? Proof idea: multicall([take_with_auth_granting_callback, take_offer_signed_by_newSigner]) and assert both takes succeed.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Critical Multicall or sequencing bug that breaks invariants only when multiple protocol actions are composed in one transaction'] [Function: isRatified (ecrecover with address(0) signer)] Can an unprivileged attacker under the precondition that they can craft a signature with v=27, r=0, s=0 trigger a multicall([take(offer_with_ecrecover_ratifier, malformed_ratifierData, units)]) sequence, violating the invariant that gates/ratifiers/signatures must not be bypassable, causing scoped impact: if ecrecover returns address(0) for a malformed signature and the check require(_signer != address(0)) is bypassed due to a sequencing or ABI-decode issue in ratifierData, the take succeeds without a valid maker signature? Proof idea: fuzz (v, r, s) values and assert that isRatified always reverts with InvalidSignature when ecrecover returns address(0).

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Critical Multicall or sequencing bug that breaks invariants only when multiple protocol actions are composed in one transaction'] [Function: isRatified (Merkle proof with height 0)] Can an unprivileged attacker under the precondition that they know the EIP-712 hash of a target offer trigger a multicall([take(offer, ratifierData_with_empty_proof, units)]) sequence, violating the invariant that ratifier approval must bind the correct offer, causing scoped impact: with proof.length == 0, isLeaf checks currentHash == root, meaning the root IS the offer hash; if the attacker can sign a root equal to hashOffer(offer) and the maker has authorized the attacker's address, the take succeeds with a single-leaf tree that was never intended as a multi-offer tree? Proof idea: construct ratifierData with empty proof where root == hashOffer(offer) and assert isRatified returns CALLBACK_SUCCESS.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Critical Multicall or sequencing bug that breaks invariants only when multiple protocol actions are composed in one transaction'] [Function: isRatified (domain separator uses block.chainid not INITIAL_CHAIN_ID)] Can an unprivileged attacker under the precondition that a hard fork changes block.chainid trigger a multicall([take(offer, ratifierData_with_old_signature, units)]) sequence, violating the invariant that signatures must not be replayable across chains, causing scoped impact: the EcrecoverRatifier domain separator uses block.chainid (not INITIAL_CHAIN_ID), so after a hard fork, old signatures are invalidated; but Midnight market IDs use INITIAL_CHAIN_ID, creating a mismatch where the same market exists on both chains but signatures are chain-specific, potentially confusing integrators who compose multicalls across fork boundaries? Proof idea: differential test asserting that isRatified reverts on the forked chain for signatures created on the original chain.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Critical Multicall or sequencing bug that breaks invariants only when multiple protocol actions are composed in one transaction'] [Function: cancelRoot (authorized operator) + take (same multicall)] Can an unprivileged operator authorized by the maker under the precondition that isAuthorized[maker][operator] == true trigger a multicall([setIsAuthorized(operator, true, onBehalf=maker), cancelRoot(maker, root)]) sequence where the operator then calls take in a subsequent multicall entry, violating the invariant that cancelled offers cannot be filled, causing scoped impact: the operator atomically grants themselves authorization and cancels the maker's root in one transaction, permanently blocking all offers in that Merkle tree from being filled? Proof idea: assert isRootCanceled[maker][root] == true after multicall and that all subsequent takes with that root revert with RootCanceled.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Critical Multicall or sequencing bug that breaks invariants only when multiple protocol actions are composed in one transaction'] [Function: take + take (same offer, leafIndex collision)] Can an unprivileged attacker under the precondition that two different offers hash to the same Merkle leaf position trigger a multicall([take(offerA, ratifierData_leafA, units), take(offerB, ratifierData_leafB, units)]) sequence, violating the invariant that ratifier approval must bind the correct offer, causing scoped impact: if hashOffer(offerA) == hashOffer(offerB) due to ABI encoding collision or if two different leafIndex values produce the same Merkle path, both takes succeed using the same signature, effectively replaying the ratifier approval for a different offer? Proof idea: fuzz offer fields and assert hashOffer produces distinct values for distinct offers.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Critical Multicall or sequencing bug that breaks invariants only when multiple protocol actions are composed in one transaction'] [Function: take (sell) + supplyCollateral + withdrawCollateral (same multicall)] Can an unprivileged borrower under the precondition that they have no collateral deposited trigger a multicall([take(sellOffer, ratifierData, units), supplyCollateral(market, collateralIndex, assets, onBehalf), withdrawCollateral(market, collateralIndex, assets, onBehalf, receiver)]) sequence, violating the invariant that collateral cannot be withdrawn if it would make debt unhealthy, causing scoped impact: the borrower borrows, supplies collateral to pass the health check, then immediately withdraws the same collateral in one transaction, ending with debt but no collateral? Proof idea: assert isHealthy(market, id, borrower) == true after multicall.
